207	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
208	#
209	```
210	
211	**Cross-module references need no per-file directive.** Files that reference cross-module `Z*_/RB*_` readonly state trip SC2153 (possible misspelling) and SC2154 (referenced but not assigned). Both are suppressed globally in `busc_shellcheckrc`, applied on the canonical lint path (the project's shellcheck tabtarget always passes `--rcfile`). No inline directive is carried — see **Shellcheck Integration** and **Prohibited Constructs § Inline Shellcheck Directives**.
212	
213	The `set -u` clause of `set -euo pipefail` is the runtime backstop for the same typo class SC2153 catches statically: a misspelled cross-module reference dies at first reference rather than silently expanding to empty.
214	
215	### Tabtarget Path Indirection
216	
217	A tabtarget in `tt/` is a colophon, not a program — it names *what* to run, never *how* to reach it. A single trampoline, `tt/z-launcher.sh`, owns the "how." Every other tabtarget is a declarative stub: a shebang, a `BURD_*` config block, and a byte-identical `exec` into the trampoline.
218	
219	```bash
220	#!/bin/bash
221	export BURD_LAUNCHER=launcher.«workbench»_workbench.sh
222	exec "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/z-launcher.sh" "${0##*/}" "${@}"
223	```
224	
225	The exec line carries no launcher token — it is identical, character for character, across every tabtarget. All per-tabtarget variation lives in the `BURD_*` block between the shebang and the exec: which launcher (`BURD_LAUNCHER`, always the first config line), and any dispatch flags (`BURD_NO_LOG`, `BURD_INTERACTIVE`) that follow.
226	
227	The trampoline has exactly two responsibilities:
228	
229	1. **Resolve the moorings location.** The consumer's configuration tree (launchers, regimes) lives under a moorings directory whose path the trampoline holds as a single hardcoded literal. This is the single point of customization at the tabtarget layer — the parallel to BURC at the CLI layer. Move the moorings, edit one line; every tabtarget follows.
230	2. **Normalize cwd to repo root.** The trampoline `cd`s to the repo root before dispatching, so every workbench starts from a deterministic directory regardless of where the user invoked the tabtarget. Downstream code relies on a known cwd rather than reconstructing one.
231	
232	**The launcher name.** `BURD_LAUNCHER` is a bare launcher basename — `launcher.<id>_workbench.sh`. Every launcher co-locates in the moorings launcher directory (`rbml_launchers/`), so the trampoline resolves the basename directly against that one directory and exec's it, forwarding the tabtarget basename and user args unchanged. `BURD_LAUNCHER` is not a private positional convention: it is a regime variable — declared in `burd_regime`, allowlisted in `bud_dispatch` — so the launcher identity travels the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value, and flows downstream unchanged.
233	
234	Why name the launcher in a config variable rather than pass it as a positional dispatch token? **Because the byte-identical exec line is load-bearing.** When the only thing that varies between two tabtargets is their `BURD_*` block, a tabtarget is purely declarative — it states *which launcher and which flags*, and holds no dispatch logic that can drift from the trampoline's expectations. The launcher identity rides the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value rather than a parallel argument slot, and the exec collapses to a true constant the qualifier checks against a single literal.
235	
236	This indirection is the tabtarget-layer instance of CLI as Module Gateway: a tabtarget is external code, and it reaches module functionality only through a launcher the trampoline resolves — never by sourcing or kindling directly. The launcher → dispatch chain it hands off to is what provides the Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables described next.
237	
238	### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables
239	
240	Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():
241	
242	| Variable | Source | Value |
243	|----------|--------|-------|
244	| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
245	| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
246	| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |
247	
248	These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.
249	
250	### Template 2: CLI Entry Point
251	
252	BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars — furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.
253	
254	```bash
255	# «shebang»
256	# «copyright»
257	
258	set -euo pipefail
259	
260	source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
261	
262	z«prefix»_furnish() {
263	
264	  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
265	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
266	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
267	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
268	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
269	
270	  # Source all dependencies
271	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
272	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"
273	
274	  # Source regime files, if any
275	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
276	
277	  z«prefix»_kindle
278	  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
279	}
280	
281	buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"
282	
283	# eof
284	```
285	
286	### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
287	
288	The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
289	
290	**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
291	
292	```bash
293	z«prefix»_furnish() {
294	  local z_command="${1:-}"
295	
296	  # Document environment variables
297	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
298	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
299	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
300	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
301	
302	  # Always source light dependencies
303	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
304	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"
305	
306	  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
307	  case "${z_command}" in
308	    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
309	      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
310	      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
311	      zheavy_kindle
312	      ;;
313	    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
314	      # These commands work with config only
315	      ;;
316	  esac
317	
318	  z«prefix»_kindle
319	}
320	```
321	
322	**Key points:**
323	- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
324	- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
325	- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
326	- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
327	- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
328	
329	**Benefits:**
330	- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
331	- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
332	- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
333	- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure
334	
335	### Template 3: Implementation Module
336	
337	```bash
338	# «shebang»
339	# «copyright»
340	
341	set -euo pipefail
342	
343	# Multiple inclusion detection
344	test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
345	Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1
346	
347	# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
348	# «PREFIX»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
349	# «PREFIX»_some_prefix="prefix_"
350	
351	######################################################################
352	# Internal Functions (z«prefix»_*)
353	
354	z«prefix»_kindle() {
355	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
356	
357	  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
358	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
359	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
360	  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps
361	
362	  # Validate module specific environment
363	  buv_file_exists "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present
364	
365	  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
366	  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"
367	
368	  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
369	  «PREFIX»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"
370	
371	  # Kindle constants (internal)
372	  Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
373	  Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_result.json"
374	  Z«PREFIX»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
375	  Z«PREFIX»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use
376	
377	  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
378	  Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"
379	
380	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
381	}
382	
383	z«prefix»_sentinel() {
384	  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
385	}
386	
387	# Helper functions follow
388	
389	######################################################################
390	# External Functions («prefix»_*)
391	
392	«prefix»_«command»() {
393	  z«prefix»_sentinel
394	
395	  local z_param1="${1:-}"
396	  local z_param2="${2:-}"
397	
398	  # Documentation block
399	  buc_doc_brief "«one_line_description»"
400	  buc_doc_param "«required_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
401	  buc_doc_oparm "«optional_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
402	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
403	
404	  # Implementation
405	  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"
406	
407	  buc_step "«user_visible_milestone»"
408	  buc_log_args "«detailed_progress_information»"
409	  [command] || buc_die "«failure_explanation»"
410	
411	  buc_success "«success_message»"
412	}
413	```
414	
415	**Kindle constant — anti-pattern:**
416	
417	```bash
418	# ❌ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
419	z«prefix»_setup() {
420	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
421	}
422	
423	# ✅ Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
424	z«prefix»_kindle() {
425	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
426	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
427	}
428	```
429	
430	**Tinder vs kindle constant — choosing correctly:**
431	
432	```bash
433	# ✅ Tinder constant: pure string, available at source time
434	«PREFIX»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name — no kindle needed
435	
436	# ✅ Tinder-on-tinder composition: expansion of earlier tinder constant
437	«PREFIX»_role_director="director"
438	«PREFIX»_fact_ext_director="ext-${«PREFIX»_role_director}"  # resolves at source time
439	
440	# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
441	«PREFIX»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name — kindle required
442	
443	# ❌ Wrong: runtime variable expansion in tinder position
444	«PREFIX»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: BURD_TEMP_DIR is runtime — must be kindle
445	
446	# ❌ Wrong: computation in tinder position
447	«PREFIX»_color_red=$(z«prefix»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() — must be in kindle
448	
449	# ❌ Wrong: expansion of kindle constant in tinder position
450	«PREFIX»_derived="${«PREFIX»_KINDLED_VAR}/sub"  # VIOLATION: KINDLED_VAR available only after kindle
451	```
452	
453	## Variable Handling (General Rules)
454	
455	### Expansion Requirements
456	
457	**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
458	```bash
459	"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
460	"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
461	"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
462	```
463	
464	**Never use:**
465	```bash
466	$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
467	"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
468	${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
469	```
470	
471	**No tilde in quoted strings** — tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
472	```bash
473	# ❌ Tilde does not expand in quotes
474	"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory
475	
476	# ✅ Use $HOME instead
477	"${HOME}/Downloads/file.txt"    # Expands correctly
478	```
479	
480	**Quote inside parameter expansion operators:**
481	```bash
482	# ✅ Inner expansion quoted separately — matches literally
483	"${z_path%"${z_suffix}"}"       # Strip suffix
484	"${z_path#"${z_prefix}"}"       # Strip prefix
485	
486	# ❌ Inner expansion unquoted — matches as a glob pattern
487	"${z_path%${z_suffix}}"         # z_suffix with * or ? chars matches as glob!
488	"${z_path#${z_prefix}}"         # Same risk
489	```
490	
491	When using `%`, `%%`, `#`, `##` operators with a variable as the pattern, the inner expansion must be quoted separately. Without inner quotes, the pattern is glob-expanded — a suffix containing `*` or `?` silently matches more than intended.
492	
493	### Assignment Patterns
494	
495	```bash
496	# Direct assignment
497	local z_message="Processing item"
498	
499	# ❌ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
500	local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!
501	
502	# ✅ ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
503	local z_name=""
504	local z_value="default"
505	local z_result=0
506	
507	# ❌ NEVER use local -i — silently coerces non-integer values to 0
508	local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 — violates no-silent-failures!
509	
510	# ✅ Use plain local with explicit validation
511	local z_count="${z_input}"
512	test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"
513	
514	# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
515	local z_token
516	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
517	
518	# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
519	local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!
520	
521	# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
522	local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
523	test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}"
524	
525	# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
526	# ❌ local z_content
527	# ❌ z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity
528	
529	# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
530	local z_token
531	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
532	
533	# Temp files INSTEAD of command substitution holds intermediate results
534	echo "${z_result}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE}"
535	complex_command > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
536	process < "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
537	local z_result=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}")
538	test -n "${z_result}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}"
539	```
540	
541	### Prefer Bash Builtins Over External Tools
542	
543	```bash
544	# ✅ String manipulation with parameter expansion
545	"${z_path##*/}"                    # Instead of: basename
546	"${z_path%/*}"                     # Instead of: dirname
547	"${z_string//old/new}"             # Instead of: sed 's/old/new/g'
548	"${z_string#prefix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/^prefix//'
549	"${z_string%suffix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/suffix$//'
550	
551	# ✅ Reading lines with 'read' — guard handles missing trailing newline
552	while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
553	  echo "Processing: ${z_line}" || buc_die "Failed to echo line"
554	done < "${Z_MODULE_INPUT_FILE}"
555	
556	# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
557	z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
558	```
559	
560	### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution
561	
562	All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.
563	
564	These rules govern **command substitution** — `$(command)`, which spawns a subshell, runs a command, and can fail. They do not apply to `$(<file)`, which despite sharing the `$()` syntax is a bash **builtin file redirect**: no subshell, no command, nothing to fail. `$(<file)` needs no `|| buc_die` guard — it takes content validation (`test -n` on the result) instead, since a successful read of an empty or missing file still yields the empty string.
565	
566	```bash
567	# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
568	z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!
569	
570	# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
571	z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!
572	
573	# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
574	complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
575	process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
576	read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"
577	
578	# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
579	date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
580	local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
581	test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"
582	
583	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
584	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
585	```
586	
587	**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.
588	
589	### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress
590	
591	Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
592	
593	```bash
594	# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
595	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
596	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"
597	
598	# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
599	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
600	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
601	```
602	
603	**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.
604	
605	**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:
606	
607	```bash
608	# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
609	Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"
610	
611	# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
612	local z_index=0
613	while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
614	  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
615	  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"
616	
617	  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
618	    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
619	    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
620	
621	  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
622	done
623	```
624	
625	### String Usage
626	
627	```bash
628	# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
629	jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
630	grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"
631	
632	# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
633	```
634	
635	### Readonly Patterns
636	
637	Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:
638	
639	#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables
640	
641	Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.
642	
643	```bash
644	# ✅ Default: readonly locals
645	local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
646	local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
647	local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
648	local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
649	```
650	
651	**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:
652	
653	```bash
654	# Exception 1: Loop counters
655	local z_index=0
656	z_index=$((z_index + 1))
657	
658	# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
659	local z_manifest_file=""
660	for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
661	  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
662	  ...
663	done
664	
665	# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
666	local z_token
667	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
668	
669	# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
670	local z_any_failed=0
671	local z_current_group=""
672	```
673	
674	**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.
675	
676	#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant
677	
678	Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:
679	
680	```bash
681	z«prefix»_kindle() {
682	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
683	
684	  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
685	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
686	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3
687	
688	  # Computed from already-set values
689	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
690	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
691	
692	  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
693	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"
694	
695	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
696	}
697	```
698	
699	If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.
700	
701	**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.
702	
703	#### Mutable Kindle State
704	
705	Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.
706	
707	**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.
708	
709	```bash
710	z«prefix»_kindle() {
711	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
712	
713	  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
714	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
715	
716	  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
717	  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
718	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
719	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment
720	
721	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
722	}
723	```
724	
725	**Rules**:
726	- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
727	- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
728	- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
729	- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)
730	
731	**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.
732	
733	```bash
734	# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
735	z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
736	  z«prefix»_sentinel
737	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
738	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
739	  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
740	}
741	```
742	
743	**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
744	```bash
745	# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
746	# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
747	# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
748	#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
749	```
750	
751	#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce
752	
753	For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):
754	
755	```bash
756	z«prefix»_enforce() {
757	  z«prefix»_sentinel
758	  buv_vet «SCOPE»
759	  # ... custom format checks ...
760	}
761	
762	# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
763	# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
764	readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE
765	
766	# Derived state built from validated values:
767	Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
768	```
769	
770	**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.
771	
772	## Special Function Definitions
773	
774	### Special Function Patterns
775	
776	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
777	
778	| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
779	|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
780	| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
781	| Capture   | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_«source») | Clean error handling, single return |
782	| Enroll    | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars | No (use buc_log_«source») | Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) in kindle only; returns via variables |
783	| Recite    | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`    | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout or exit 1             | No (use buc_log_«source») | Read-only access to rolls; never mutates |
784	| Fixture baste | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`         | —                   | — (side effects only)        | Optional                  | Fixture subshell; kindle, source, configure |
785	| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
786	| Yawp      | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `z«module»_sentinel` | `z_«module»_«group»` var    | No                        | Pure assignment, cannot fail, no output |
787	
788	---
789	
790	#### Capture Functions (controlled command substitution)
791	
792	**Purpose: Return a single processed value through stdout with clean error handling.**
793	
794	Capture functions encapsulate command substitution to ensure predictable error handling and single-point-of-return semantics.
795	
796	```bash
797	z«prefix»_«name»_capture() {
798	  z«prefix»_sentinel
799	
800	  # Process and validate internally
801	  local z_result
802	  z_result=$(some_command | process) || return 1
803	
804	  # Validate without buc_die
805	  test -n "${z_result}" || return 1
806	
807	  # Single output at end
808	  echo "${z_result}"
809	}
810	
811	# Caller uses two-line pattern
812	local z_value
813	z_value=$(z«prefix»_«name»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
814	```
815	
816	**Use capture functions for:**
817	- Secrets that must never touch disk
818	- Values requiring command substitution or pipeline processing
819	- Any computed value where clean error handling matters
820	
821	**Contract:**
822	- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
823	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
824	- No side effects beyond the returned value
825	- May source credential files when necessary for security
826	- May use `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail (writes to transcript file only)
827	- Never writes secrets to disk
828	- Never writes to stderr
829	
830	#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)
831	
832	Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.
833	
834	```bash
835	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate() {
836	  z«prefix»_sentinel
837	
838	  local z_file="${1:-}"
839	
840	  # Return status only - no output, no dies
841	  test -f "${z_file}" || return 1
842	  grep -q "required_pattern" "${z_file}" || return 1
843	
844	  return 0
845	}
846	
847	# Caller uses in conditionals
848	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate "${z_config}" || buc_warn "Config invalid, using defaults"
849	```
850	
851	#### Enroll Functions (kindle-only registry population)
852	
853	**Purpose**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during module initialization. Enroll functions solve the subshell problem: when a function both mutates shared state and returns a value, calling it in `$()` loses the mutations.
854	
855	**The parallel-array registry pattern (rolls)**:
856	- Kindle initializes N empty arrays (rolls) of the same length
857	- Enroll validates inputs and appends atomically to all rolls
858	- Recite functions (see below) provide read-only access after kindle completes
859	- Variable naming: `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` for all roll arrays (internal state, initialized in kindle)
860	
861	**Example** (using synthetic notation — «reg» owns the rolls, «con» consumes them):
862	
863	```bash
864	# Module «reg» — owns the rolls
865	z«reg»_kindle() {
866	  test -z "${Z«REG»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
867	
868	  z_«reg»_name_roll=()
869	  z_«reg»_target_roll=()
870	  z_«reg»_handler_roll=()
871	
872	  Z«REG»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last — sentinel guards all subsequent calls
873	}
874	
875	# Enroll function — called by consuming module's kindle, NOT «reg»'s own kindle
876	«reg»_enroll() {
877	  z«reg»_sentinel   # Safe: «reg» is already kindled when consumer calls this
878	
879	  local z_name="${1:-}"
880	  local z_target="${2:-}"
881	  local z_handler="${3:-}"
882	  test -n "${z_name}" || buc_die "enroll: name required"
883	
884	  # Registration-time validation
885	  declare -F "${z_handler}" >/dev/null || buc_die "enroll: handler not found: ${z_handler}"
886	
887	  z_«reg»_name_roll+=("${z_name}")
888	  z_«reg»_target_roll+=("${z_target}")
889	  z_«reg»_handler_roll+=("${z_handler}")
890	
891	  # Return via variable — NOT echo
892	  z_«reg»_enroll_name="${z_name}"
893	}
894	
895	# Module «con» — consumes the registry
896	z«con»_kindle() {
897	  test -z "${Z«CON»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
898	
899	  # «reg» must be kindled first (rolls initialized, sentinel passes)
900	  «reg»_enroll "alpha" "/path/alpha" "handle_alpha"
901	  Z«CON»_DEFAULT="${z_«reg»_enroll_name}"
902	
903	  «reg»_enroll "bravo" "/path/bravo" "handle_bravo"
904	
905	  Z«CON»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last
906	}
907	```
908	
909	**Key constraints**:
910	- Enroll functions may ONLY be called within kindle
911	- Rolls are initialized in kindle, populated by enroll in kindle, then immutable after kindle completes
912	- Enroll functions work exclusively with rolls (parallel arrays) — no other shared state mutation
913	- Registration-time validation: check invariants at enroll time, not at recite/execution time
914	- Return values use `z_«funcname»_«retval»` convention (function name embedded verbatim for traceability)
915	- Must NOT be called inside `$()` — side effects would be lost
916	- May use `buc_die` internally (unlike `_capture`)
917	
918	**Two-level registries**: When entities have parent-child relationships (e.g., groups containing items), use two flat registries with a foreign-key column rather than per-parent dynamic arrays. This avoids `eval` and stays bash 3.2 safe.
919	
920	**Contract summary**:
921	- Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) only
922	- Returns value(s) via `z_«funcname»_«retval»` variables (NOT echo)
923	- Called exclusively within kindle
924	- Should rarely be used — only when a function must both mutate and return
925	
926	#### Recite Functions (read-only roll access)
927	
928	**Purpose: Provide read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions.**
929	
930	Recite functions are the only access path to rolls after kindle completes. They must never mutate roll arrays.
931	
932	```bash
933	«prefix»_«what»_recite() {
934	  z«prefix»_sentinel
935	
936	  local z_key="${1:-}"
937	  test -n "${z_key}" || return 1
938	
939	  local z_i=0
940	  for z_i in "${!z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
941	    test "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_key}" || continue
942	    echo "${z_«prefix»_target_roll[$z_i]}" || return 1
943	    return 0
944	  done
945	  return 1
946	}
947	
948	# Caller uses two-line capture pattern
949	local z_target
950	z_target=$(«prefix»_target_recite "alpha") || buc_die "not found"
951	```
952	
953	**Contract:**
954	- Read-only: MUST NOT mutate roll arrays or any shared state
955	- Returns via echo (like capture functions) — safe to call in `$()`
956	- Returns 1 on not-found (like `_capture`) — never uses `buc_die` internally
957	- Caller decides error handling: `|| buc_die` or conditional
958	- Usable anywhere after kindle completes (not restricted to kindle)
959	
960	#### Yawp Functions (pure-computation group return)
961	
962	**Purpose: Produce a value into a shared group return variable via pure bash assignment. No subshells, no stdout, no stderr.**
963	
964	Named for Whitman's "barbaric yawp" — a loud, unmistakeable declaration into a known place. Yawp functions solve the high-frequency value-production problem: when dozens of calls per function each need a computed string, `$()` subshell overhead is disproportionate to the work being done.
965	
966	```bash
967	# «prefix»_«name»_yawp arg1 [arg2] — what it stamps
968	# → local -r z_name="${z_«module»_«group»}"
969	«prefix»_«name»_yawp() {
970	  z«module»_sentinel
971	  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_PREFIX}${1:-}${ZMOD_SUFFIX}"
972	}
973	```
974	
975	The two-line comment is the per-function template: signature on line 1, mandatory capture pattern on line 2. The `→` arrow is a visual reminder that the caller must capture on the same line.
976	
977	**Return variable**: `z_«module»_«group»` — mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle. One per function group. All `_yawp` functions in a group share the same return variable. The value is valid until the next `_yawp` call in the same group.
978	
979	```bash
980	# In kindle — initialize mutable kindle state for yawp group
981	z_«module»_«group»=""
982	
983	# In function — pure assignment, no process spawning
984	«prefix»_cmd_yawp() {
985	  z«module»_sentinel
986	  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_MARKER_CMD}${1:-}${ZMOD_MARKER_END}"
987	}
988	
989	# Caller pattern — semicolon capture on same line
990	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
991	«prefix»_ui_yawp "Save";         local -r z_ui="${z_«module»_«group»}"
992	```
993	
994	**Contract:**
995	- Sets `z_«module»_«group»` (mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle)
996	- **Caller MUST capture to `local -r` on the same line** — semicolon-separated, value is destroyed by the next `_yawp` call in the same group
997	- Pure bash builtins only — no external commands, no process spawning
998	- Cannot fail — no `|| buc_die` needed at call site
999	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
1000	- No stdout, no stderr
1001	- First line: sentinel check
1002	
1003	**Capture discipline — the return variable is a transient slot, not a durable value:**
1004	
1005	```bash
1006	# CORRECT — semicolon capture on same line, compose from captured locals
1007	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
1008	«prefix»_tt_yawp "«colophon»";      local -r z_tt="${z_«module»_«group»}"
1009	buh_line "Run ${z_cmd} to validate: ${z_tt}"
1010	
1011	# AVOID — two-line form works but doesn't enforce discipline
1012	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
1013	local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
1014	# ↑ Nothing prevents inserting code between yawp and capture.
1015	# The semicolon form makes interleaving structurally impossible.
1016	
1017	# WRONG — z_«group» used directly in output (fragile, unreadable)
1018	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
1019	buh_line "Run ${z_«module»_«group»}"
1020	
1021	# WRONG — two yawps before capture (first value lost)
1022	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
1023	«prefix»_tt_yawp "«colophon»"
1024	buh_line "${z_«module»_«group»}"  # only has tt, cmd is gone
1025	```
1026	
1027	The semicolon makes the capture discipline visually self-enforcing — you cannot accidentally insert code between the yawp and its capture. The two-line form invited interleaving mistakes; the one-line form eliminates them structurally.
1028	
1029	The one-yawp-one-capture rhythm is the invariant. Pre-captured constants (e.g., `RBYC_*` from `zrbyc_kindle`) are safe to interpolate directly because they were captured at kindle time — the discipline applies at the yawp call site, not at the interpolation site.
1030	
1031	**Use yawp functions for:**
1032	- Pure string assembly (concatenation, marker stamping)
1033	- Value production where `$()` subshell overhead is unnecessary
1034	- High-frequency call sites (dozens of calls per function)
1035	- Any computed value that uses only bash builtins and cannot fail
1036

<!--
